Summary: This study developed a radiomic classifier using contrast-enhanced T1-weighted and perfusion MRI images to differentiate pseudoprogression from recurrent tumor in patients with IDH wild-type glioblastoma. The classifier, enhanced with MGMT methylation status, achieved an AUC of 0.89 in cross-validation, suggesting its potential to improve diagnostic accuracy and inform treatment decisions.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to any specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study clearly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, aiming for a representative cohort of IDH wild-type glioblastoma patients.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is histopathology, which is a robust and well-established method for differentiating between recurrent tumor and pseudoprogression.

Item4.reason: The study uses data from a single institution.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source (contrast-enhanced T1-weighted and DCE perfusion MRI) reflects established standardization approaches as part of the institutional standard of care for glioma imaging.

Item6.reason: The study provides details of MRI acquisition parameters in the supplemental methods.

Item7.reason: The study mentions that MRI scans were acquired within 6 weeks before repeat resection, which is appropriate to validate the presence or absence of target conditions at the moment of the diagnostic imaging exams.

Condition1.reason: The study involves segmentation of enhancing lesions on MRI for radiomic feature extraction.

Condition2.reason: The study does not explicitly state whether the segmentation was fully automated. The supplemental methods would need to be reviewed to confirm.

Item8.reason: The study mentions details of MRI acquisition and segmentation in the supplemental methods.

Item9.reason: The study does not explicitly state whether the segmentation was fully automated, so this is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The study does not specify whether the test set segmentation masks were produced by a single reader or automated tool.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts hand-crafted radiomic features using CERR software.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (first order histogram features and second order features using gray level correlation matrix, gray level size zone, gray level run length matrix, shape-based metrics, and edge descriptors including Sobel and directional Gabor edge filters) and mentions using default settings for feature extraction.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of radiomic features.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline.
